title: Pronunciation Practice 2: The Letter D and Nasals
description: Master the difference between the dental and retroflex D sounds, and learn about nasalization.

blocks.title: The 'D' Sounds and Nasals
points: Soft 'd' (द): Tip of the tongue on the back of the front teeth (like Spanish 'dos'). Example: दो (Do - Two).
Hard 'D' (ड): Curled tongue on the roof of the mouth (like English 'door').
Nasal sounds (ं or ँ): Hindi has many nasalized vowels. Air goes through the nose AND mouth simultaneously.
Example of nasal: हूँ (Hoon - Am/Are) or हाँ (Haan - Yes).

blocks.title: The Two D's
blocks.instruction: The 'D' row is crucial for English speakers. Pay close attention.

pairs.diff: Notice how English naturally uses the retroflex D, but Hindi uses both!

words.note: Yes. Say 'Haa' and let air escape through your nose.
I/Me. 'mai' + nasal n. Similar to French 'un'.

items.statement: The Hindi 'd' in 'das' (ten) is pronounced exactly like the 'd' in English 'dog'.
items.explanation: No, the 'd' in 'das' is dental (on the teeth). The English 'd' is alveolar (behind the teeth), closer to the Hindi hard 'D'.

items.statement: Words with a nasal ending like 'hãã' (yes) require air flow through the nose.
